title: Top Five Apocalypse Movies, Plus the Return of James Gray
format: Sean and Amanda each rank their five favorite apocalypse movies, then James Gray returns to talk lockdown filmmaking, Ad Astra, Armageddon Time, and what he's been showing his kids.

films.title: She Dies Tomorrow
films.note: Amy Seimetz's self-funded, self-shot movie about a contagious anxiety that kills; the new release that made apocalypse the week's topic. Sean's interview with Seimetz runs the following week.

films.title: Starship Troopers
films.note: Sean's other short-list pick. He loved it at 15 as pure alien-invasion spectacle before clocking the Third Reich satire underneath, and says giant bugs specifically are what would break him.

films.title: The Andromeda Strain
films.note: Amanda watched this Michael Crichton adaptation for the first time to prep. A satellite crash-lands and infects a New Mexico town, but the back two-thirds are pure lab procedural, which she pegs as Soderbergh's evident model for Contagion, alongside All the President's Men.

films.title: Mad Max: Beyond Thunderdome
films.note: Sean rewatched it this week to confirm he still doesn't like it, and doesn't think it holds up next to Fury Road.

films.title: The Witches of Eastwick
films.note: Sean's quarantine discovery, prompted by a You Must Remember This deep dive on production designer Polly Platt; he traces George Miller's visual moves from here through to Fury Road.

films.title: Melancholia
films.note: No. 1. Right before Amanda walked down the aisle at her wedding, her dad turned to her and said, "This is just like the wedding in Melancholia": meant, and received, as a compliment.

films.title: Mad Max: Fury Road
films.note: No. 2. Not a genre she usually gravitates to, but picked for pure apocalypse imagery: the vista, the desperation, and the exact way she pictures surviving without her contacts, which is to say not surviving at all.

films.title: Arrival
films.note: No. 3. Denis Villeneuve's first-contact drama, picked for the long stretch where nobody, including the audience, knows whether this is even the apocalypse.

films.title: Contagion
films.note: No. 4. Rewatched four months into COVID-19. Some of it, like Kate Winslet's contact tracing, plays as prophecy; some of it, like the vaccine timeline, plays as movie magic.

films.title: Independence Day
films.note: No. 5. Her one mandatory Roland Emmerich/Will Smith pick, chosen mostly for the laser beam over the White House and Bill Pullman's speech.

films.title: Stalker
films.note: No. 1. Andrei Tarkovsky's film about a guide leading two men into the Zone, a post-apocalyptic space that grants wishes. Sean first saw it at 19 and didn't get it; he rewatched it this week and was floored.

films.title: Terminator 2: Judgment Day
films.note: No. 2. James Cameron's sequel, picked for Sarah Connor's playground nuclear-blast nightmare, the image Sean says defined the apocalypse for him as a kid.

films.title: WALL·E
films.note: No. 3. Andrew Stanton's Pixar film starts as a post-apocalyptic story about an abandoned Earth and turns into an indictment of the fat, phone-addicted humans who fled it.

films.title: Dr. Strangelove or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb
films.note: No. 4. Kubrick's nuclear farce, picked as the counter to the slow-burn apocalypses elsewhere on the list: mutually assured destruction inside 24 hours.

films.title: Children of Men
films.note: No. 5. Alfonso Cuarón's film about a world that can no longer have children; Sean calls it Cuarón's best. He dates it to 2007 on air, but it's a 2006 release.

segments.heading: James Gray on Ad Astra, quarantine, and what's next

films.title: Ad Astra
films.note: His most expensive, most "big tent" film. With some distance, he says he's still processing mixed reactions and didn't have full creative control over the final cut, unlike his earlier films.

films.title: The Lost City of Z
films.note: Sean's reference point for Gray's prior work, alongside Ad Astra and The Yards.

films.title: The Yards
films.note: Sean's reference point for Gray's earlier New York-set films.

films.title: The Immigrant
films.note: Gray's own example of a film that's personal without being autobiographical, about a woman in 1921.

films.title: Armageddon Time
films.note: Gray finished the first draft in early March 2020, right before lockdown, with Robert De Niro, Anne Hathaway, Oscar Isaac, and Cate Blanchett attached. A personal, New York-set drama he calls the script he's most fond of to date; it wouldn't reach theaters for another two years.

segments.heading: James Gray on what he's showing his kids

films.title: Chinatown
films.note: Reading Sam Wasson's book about its making prompted Gray's broader rumination on old Hollywood right before he started rewatching classics with his kids.

films.title: The Killers
films.note: Robert Siodmak's noir with Burt Lancaster and Ava Gardner. Rewatching it mid-lockdown triggered Gray's broader Lancaster reappraisal.

films.title: It's a Gift
films.note: W.C. Fields and Baby LeRoy, watched the prior night. Prompted his son to ask what $44,000 in the film was worth today; Gray guessed a million, and it's actually about $844,000.

films.title: The Train
films.note: John Frankenheimer's Lancaster vehicle, part of a run of Lancaster titles during lockdown.

films.title: Strangers on a Train
films.note: One of several Hitchcock titles in rotation with his kids.

films.title: Rear Window
films.note: Another Hitchcock in the rotation.

films.title: Lifeboat
films.note: Hitchcock's single-set World War II thriller; his kids' favorite of the bunch.

films.title: The Graduate
films.note: Shown to his 14-year-old, who called the Anne Bancroft/Dustin Hoffman bar scene "a great shot" on his own. Gray, watching it again for the first time in 25 years, thinks the back half is weaker than the first.

films.title: A Clockwork Orange
films.note: Watched with his 14-year-old; Gray says he had to substantially revise his opinion of it on this viewing.

films.title: Gold Diggers of 1933
films.note: Currently streaming on HBO Max, which Gray flags as proof of how much easier this era of film is to access now than when he was young.

films.title: Sunset Boulevard
films.note: His closing recommendation for the moment. Gloria Swanson's isolation and hunger for connection hit differently rewatched mid-lockdown.

films.title: Taxi Driver
films.note: He wants to show it to his 14-year-old but thinks his younger daughter isn't ready for it yet.

films.title: Dog Day Afternoon
films.note: Shown to the kids; a hit.

films.title: The Last Detail
films.note: Hal Ashby's film with Jack Nicholson. Gray loves it, but says his kids couldn't follow the premise of a sailor's brief furlough before a prison sentence.

films.title: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
films.note: The older kids thought it was great; the youngest found it upsetting.

films.title: The Grapes of Wrath
films.note: Required a primer on strikes, unions, and the New Deal before his kids could follow it. Gray calls it important for them to understand what deprivation looks like.

films.title: Double Indemnity
films.note: Landed well with the kids, unlike some of the more period-specific picks.

films.title: The Swimmer
films.note: Frank Perry's film with Burt Lancaster, one of Gray's biggest lockdown discoveries. Not a perfect movie, he says, but he couldn't believe it got made.

films.title: Story of G.I. Joe
films.note: William Wellman's war film with Burgess Meredith and Robert Mitchum, a recent first watch Gray calls terrific and compares to They Were Expendable.

films.title: They Were Expendable
films.note: John Ford's film with Robert Montgomery and John Wayne, Gray's point of comparison for The Story of G.I. Joe.

films.title: The Leopard
films.note: Visconti's three-hour-plus epic with Lancaster, Gray's example of a film that's genuinely work to watch, even when it's great.

films.title: Amarcord
films.note: Fellini's dreamlike memoir. Gray cites the black-garbage-bag ocean-liner scene as the kind of heightened style American realism can't get away with.

films.title: Bullfighter and the Lady
films.note: Budd Boetticher's film, which Gray mentioned on a prior appearance on the show and finally watched during lockdown.

films: All Quiet on the Western Front
